championshipClue:
Founded by seafaring traders, this city became one of the Mediterranean’s greatest powers.
Though later destroyed by Rome, its legacy survives in famous explorers, alphabetic influences, and a legendary queen whose name still appears in literature today.
clues: This capital, whose Māori name means “the great harbor of Tara,” sits on a fault line and is known for its fierce winds whipping through Cook Strait.
This city, once the U.S. capital, is home to Independence Hall, where both the Declaration and Constitution were debated.
This spooky sitcom family lived at 1313 Mockingbird Lane and featured a Frankenstein‑like dad named Herman.

When packing a first-aid kit for countries like Japan or the UAE, travelers must leave behind over-the-counter cold medicines containing this specific decongestant ingredient, as it is classified as a banned stimulant.
Long before the arrival of private 24-hour networks, generations of Sri Lankans relied strictly on this flagship evening state news broadcast.

This real‑estate developer, known for emphasizing innovation in property planning, once said that “it starts with being creative and applying various development strategies,” a philosophy he’s repeated in interviews and community talks alike.
Once home to Phoenicians and Moors, this nation uniquely touches both the Atlantic and the Mediterranean, giving it two distinct maritime identities.

Humphrey Bogart delivered the line “Here’s looking at you, kid” in this wartime romance set in Morocco.

Modern silver prices are increasingly dictated by its status as an industrial commodity, specifically due to its massive use as a microscopic electrical conductor inside the photovoltaic cells of these green energy panels.

Presenting an existential threat to digital news publishers, this specific industry term describes Google or Perplexity search results pages that use generative AI to completely answer a query on-screen, eliminating the user's need to click a link to read the original article.
Completed in the 21st century, this tower’s design was inspired by a regional flower, and its shadow can stretch across an entire artificial lake on certain afternoons.

Long before becoming a household name in consumer advocacy, this future National Radio Hall of Fame inductee built his initial fortune in his twenties by capitalizing on Carter-era airline deregulation to launch an Atlanta-based agency chain called Action Travel. After 9/11, he added to his resume by spending 21 years as a volunteer officer in the Georgia State Defense Force, eventually retiring at the rank of Major.
Often mistaken for sails or shells, this building’s construction was so complex that its architect resigned long before its opening — yet it remains the defining silhouette of its harbor.

Built in the early 20th century, this colossal sculpture watches over a city once known for gold rushes, and its granite faces required decades of drilling and dynamite.

Charlton Heston parted the Red Sea—on screen, at least—in this 1956 biblical epic.
Taking care of your home's energy bill means ensuring your attic insulation has a high enough number on this letter-based rating scale, which measures its resistance to heat flow.

Before underwater cables spanned the English Channel, this global news agency, founded in 1851, famously used a fleet of over 45 carrier pigeons to bridge the gap in the European telegraph lines between Brussels and Aachen.
Recognized worldwide despite often being partially invisible, this structure’s signature color was originally chosen to help it stand out during wartime naval construction.
